planting.note: Plant outdoors in spring after frosts.

directions: Grow in full sun in the ground or a large pot with sharp drainage. Trim back by roughly half in early spring for a dense, well-branched plant. Bright magenta spikes flower from summer to frost and draw butterflies to small gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger ends.

directions: Plant in full sun with good drainage; it suits patio containers well. Prune back by about half in early spring to maintain a compact shape. Creamy ivory-white spikes bloom from summer into fall and are lightly fragrant.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Site in full sun with free-draining soil, in a bed or roomy container. Cut back by half in early spring to build a full, bushy form. Deep violet-purple spikes bloom from summer to frost and are richly scented.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frosts have finished.

directions: Plant in full sun to keep the leaf variegation bright and the soil well drained. Prune hard in late winter and remove any all-green reverting shoots as they appear. Cream-edged foliage sets off deep reddish-purple spikes from midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frosts end.

directions: Grow in full sun for the strongest gold-and-green leaf markings and use well-drained soil. Cut back hard in late winter and pinch out any shoots that revert to plain green. Fragrant magenta-purple spikes contrast with the bright variegated leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring, and give it a sheltered spot in colder areas.

directions: Plant this large evergreen-to-semi-evergreen shrub in full sun with shelter from cold winds. Unlike the summer butterfly bushes, it flowers on old wood, so prune only lightly right after bloom. Round orange-yellow flower clusters like small pom-poms appear in late spring and smell of honey.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it can also be trained as a small weeping standard.

directions: Give this graceful, arching shrub full sun and room for its cascading branches. It blooms on the previous year's wood, so prune immediately after flowering rather than in late winter. Fragrant lilac-purple flowers wreathe the weeping stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in an open, sunny position.

directions: Site in full sun to bring out the silky silver sheen of the narrow leaves and provide well-drained soil. Prune right after the early-summer bloom, since flowers form on last year's growth. Fragrant lilac flowers cover the arching silvery stems in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this hybrid in full sun with good drainage and space for a large, arching bush. Cut back hard in late winter to encourage strong new flowering shoots. Rounded clusters of golden-orange flowers, unusual among butterfly bushes, bloom through summer and draw butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid full sun and well-drained soil with plenty of room. Prune to a low framework in late winter for the best flowering. Clustered soft yellow, honey-scented blooms cover the arching branches in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost date.

directions: Plant in full sun and freely draining soil for the strongest two-tone display. Cut back hard in late winter to renew the flowering wood. Spikes open in a blend of peachy-yellow and lavender-pink through summer, an unusual color mix for the group.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frosts have ended.

directions: Plant this hybrid in full sun where its silvery-gray young foliage can shine, in well-drained soil. Cut back to a low framework in late winter for compact, heavy flowering. Fragrant lavender-blue spikes with orange eyes bloom over felted gray leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Give this tender-ish species a warm, sheltered site in full sun with sharp drainage. Prune back hard in spring once new growth shows, and mulch the base in cold-winter areas. White flower spikes with orange throats top the striking silvery-white woolly stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; wall shelter helps in cooler regions.

directions: Plant against a warm sunny wall in well-drained soil for the best results in cooler climates. Prune hard in spring, as it flowers on new wood. Softly felted silver-gray leaves set off fragrant lilac spikes with white eyes through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it may spread by suckers, so site with care.

directions: Plant in full sun to light shade in well-drained soil, allowing space for its arching, suckering habit. Cut back in early spring to keep the shape in bounds. Slender, curving racemes of deep violet tubular flowers dangle gracefully from summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, sunny, sheltered position.

directions: Give this large South African species full sun and free-draining soil in a frost-protected spot. It blooms on older wood, so prune lightly after flowering to shape it. Sweetly scented pale lilac flowers cover the sage-like, wrinkled gray-green foliage in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, dry, well-drained site.

directions: Plant this heat-loving desert shrub in full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil, avoiding wet feet. It needs almost no supplemental water once established and dislikes hard pruning beyond light shaping. Small round orange ball-flowers dot the silvery, felted foliage from spring through fall.

common_name: Flutterby Petite Tutti Fruitti Butterfly Bush

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with well-drained soil, suited to smaller beds and large containers. Give a light trim in spring to keep it tidy and deadhead to prolong bloom. Raspberry-pink spikes flower freely from summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out young plants in spring once hard frosts have passed and soil is workable.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained spot with the crown at soil level and water regularly until established. Cut the whole plant back to a low framework in early spring, since flowers form on the current season's growth. Fragrant blue clusters open along the stems from late summer into autumn and draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in mid to late spring after the danger of hard frost.

directions: Give this species lean, sharply drained soil and full sun for the best flowering and hardiness. Prune stems hard in spring to keep the habit dense and encourage fresh blooming wood. Dense violet-blue tufts crowd the upper leaf axils in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a spot with room for its taller, upright form.

directions: This taller, herbaceous species dies back near the ground each winter and returns from the crown, so cut old stems away in late fall or early spring. Grow it in part sun with moist but drained soil for lush foliage. Small tubular blue flowers with long protruding stamens appear in loose sprays in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a hot, dry, well-drained position.

directions: A tough, drought-loving parent of the garden hybrids, it thrives in poor gritty soil and dislikes wet feet. Trim back in early spring to renew the low, wiry framework. Narrow gray-green leaves set off sky-blue flowers borne over a long late-summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: One of the classic dark-flowered selections, prized for its deep amethyst-blue clusters against silvery foliage. Prune to about six inches in early spring so bloom-bearing new wood grows dense. Flowers cover the mound from late summer through fall and are alive with pollinators.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: A taller, vigorous selection with silvery gray leaves and clear sky-blue flowers. Cut hard in early spring to control the loose habit and boost flowering. Airy blue clusters open in late summer and hold well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: A compact, upright old favorite valued for its rich deep-blue flowers. Shear back to a low framework each spring for a tidy, floriferous mound. Deep-blue clusters smother the plant from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give full sun for the brightest foliage color.

directions: Grown chiefly for its chartreuse-to-gold leaves, which glow best in full sun. Prune to a low frame in early spring to keep the color-rich new growth compact. Lavender-blue flowers contrast against the golden foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a warm, sunny, well-drained site.

directions: A dependable selection with dark blue flowers and gray-green foliage. Cut back close to the base in spring to renew flowering wood. Dark-blue clusters open along the arching stems in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: A compact, tidy selection noted for very dark buds that open to deep cobalt-blue flowers. Trim to a low framework in early spring to keep it dense. Strongly colored buds and blooms give a long, showy late-summer display.

planting.note: Plant in spring; full sun keeps the foliage brightly golden.

directions: Sold as Sunshine Blue, this cultivar pairs bright golden-yellow leaves with vivid blue flowers. Cut back hard in early spring to fuel colorful, compact new growth. Blue clusters stand out against the gold foliage from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a sunny, free-draining bed.

directions: Marketed as Grand Bleu, this selection shows dark green foliage and rich deep-blue flowers. Prune to a low framework in early spring for a rounded, floriferous plant. Deep-blue clusters crowd the stems through late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suits smaller beds and containers.

directions: Sold as Petit Bleu, this dwarf, dense selection fits tight spaces and pots. Cut back in early spring to keep the compact form full of new flowering shoots. Dark-blue flowers cover the neat mound in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Sold as Beyond Midnight, this cultivar stands out for its glossy near-black-green leaves that resist the yellowing seen on older types. Trim to a low frame in early spring for a rounded, dense habit. Cobalt-blue flowers pop against the dark foliage from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring; light shade helps prevent leaf scorch.

directions: Grown for its striking green leaves edged in gold, this variegated selection lights up the border. Prune to a low framework in early spring to renew the brightly marked foliage. Blue flowers contrast with the gold-margined leaves in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade with good drainage.

directions: This selection carries green leaves boldly edged in creamy white for season-long interest. Cut back in early spring to encourage clean, well-marked new growth. Blue flowers set against the white-margined foliage arrive in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, sharply drained position.

directions: An older British selection with dark stems and rich violet-blue flowers over gray-green leaves. Prune close to the base in early spring to keep the plant compact and free-flowering. Deep-blue clusters open along the stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; full sun keeps foliage vivid gold.

directions: Sold as Lil Miss Sunshine, this improved golden-leaved selection resists the fading of earlier gold forms. Cut back hard in early spring to fuel bright, compact regrowth. Blue flowers glow against the yellow foliage in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part sun with room to form a broad clump.

directions: Grown mainly for its crisp white-and-green variegated foliage, this selection lights up a shaded border. It behaves like a perennial, dying back in winter and returning from the crown, so cut old stems away by spring. Pale blue flowers dot the leafy stems in late summer and early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part sun with moist, drained soil.

directions: A tall, airy selection whose small blue flowers with long stamens seem to hover like insects. Cut the herbaceous stems back near the ground in late fall or early spring. Loose sprays of blue bloom float above the foliage in late summer.

planting.note: Set out dormant nursery plants in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the surrounding soil and water deeply through the first summer to establish the roots. Prune in late winter to shape the upright, vase-like frame and encourage larger blooms on new wood. Expect a long succession of five-petaled flowers from midsummer into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring so roots settle before the summer bloom flush.

directions: Site this cultivar in full sun for the truest violet-blue color and best flower count. Dig a hole twice the width of the rootball, backfill firmly, and mulch to hold moisture. Single sky-blue flowers with a small red throat open across the canopy from July through September.

planting.note: Set out in spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Give this near-sterile selection an open sunny spot where its large pure-white flowers show off. Water evenly during dry spells so blooms do not stall. The wavy-petaled white flowers stay open into the evening and continue from midsummer until frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring once hard frosts have passed.

directions: Choose a full-sun location and enrich poor soil with compost at planting. Keep young shrubs weed-free and watered until established, then reduce watering. White flowers with a deep reddish-purple eye zone appear from July onward.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring into moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in full sun and water regularly the first year to build a strong framework. A late-winter trim keeps the shrub tidy and improves flowering. Rich rose-pink flowers with a dark red center bloom heavily from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in spring.

directions: Position this vigorous selection in full sun with room to reach its mature width. Mulch the root zone and water during droughts to keep buds forming. Lavender-pink flowers with a red throat open in a long summer-to-autumn display.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this double-flowered form a sunny, sheltered spot and steady moisture. Prune lightly in late winter to keep the branches from flopping under heavy bloom. Soft pink pompon-like double flowers cover the plant through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last hard freeze.

directions: Set in full sun in average, well-drained soil and water through establishment. Thin crowded stems in late winter to open the canopy and boost bloom size. Double lavender-purple flowers appear over a long stretch of summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in full sun and keep the soil evenly moist while roots take hold. Cut back hard in late winter for the largest flowers on the season's new growth. Large rose-pink single flowers with a crimson center bloom from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Choose a sunny site and amend heavy soil with organic matter for good drainage. Water consistently the first two summers, then only during dry weather. Double magenta-red flowers with a darker eye bloom abundantly through late summer.

planting.note: Set out in spring in fertile ground.

directions: Plant in full sun where the crisp flower color reads well against green foliage. Mulch to conserve moisture and feed lightly in spring. Pure white single flowers with a bold red center appear from July into September.

planting.note: Plant in spring for summer flowering the same year.

directions: Give this Chiffon-series shrub full sun and average well-drained soil. Water deeply but infrequently once established to keep it healthy. Powder-blue flowers with a lacy central rosette of petaloids bloom for months in summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny location.

directions: Plant in full to partial sun in soil that drains freely. Keep young plants watered and mulched through the first season. The pure white anemone-form flowers carry a frilly inner ruff and bloom all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, open bed.

directions: Set in full sun for the heaviest flowering and best form. Prune in late winter to control height and remove weak wood. Soft lavender flowers with a delicate inner rosette open from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this selection a sunny site and steady moisture for continuous bud set. Mulch the base and trim in late winter to shape. Ruffly pale-pink double flowers with a lighter center bloom across the summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into fertile soil.

directions: Plant in full sun where its saturated color stands out in the border. Water through establishment and mulch to keep roots cool. Rich magenta-pink anemone-form flowers appear over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny spot.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil and water regularly the first year. A light late-winter prune keeps the plant compact. White flowers marked with fine red veining and a lacy center bloom through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring for the first summer bloom.

directions: Grow in full sun and average soil that never stays waterlogged. Water deeply during dry stretches to sustain flowering. Pale flowers streaked with pink rays from a deep red eye open all summer long.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun for the deepest blue single flowers and least legginess. Water through the first summer and mulch the root zone. Vivid blue blooms with a red throat appear from July until frost and draw pollinators.

common_name: Azurri Blue Satin Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny bed.

directions: Choose a full-sun location and well-drained soil for the best color and near-seedless habit. Water evenly to keep buds coming through the heat. Large true-blue flowers with a red eye bloom heavily all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once frost danger passes.

directions: Plant in full sun in ordinary garden soil with good drainage. Water regularly the first season, then during dry spells only. Bright rose-pink flowers with a red center bloom continuously through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Give this upright selection full sun and room to reach its mature height. Mulch and water to establish, then let it grow on its own. Deep violet-purple flowers with a red throat cover the shrub from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into sun.

directions: Plant in full sun and enrich lean soil with compost at the start. Keep evenly moist the first year to build a robust plant. Large pink flowers with a bright pink-red eye bloom all summer and resist reseeding.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny site.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil for the strongest color. Water deeply through establishment and mulch to retain moisture. Deep purple flowers with a red center bloom through the summer heat.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun in average soil and water the first summer to establish. Prune in late winter to keep an upright, well-branched shape. Vivid magenta single flowers with a deep red throat bloom over many weeks.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile ground.

directions: Set in full sun where the soft coloring can be admired up close. Water regularly to keep the plant vigorous and free-flowering. Pale blush-pink flowers streaked with crimson from a red heart open through late summer.

common_name: Duc de Brabant Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun and support the branches if heavy double flowers weigh them down. Water evenly and mulch to keep roots cool. Rich rosy-red double flowers appear from midsummer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Grow in full sun in well-drained soil and water to establish. Prune in late winter to encourage large blooms on fresh growth. Unmarked pure white single flowers with no central eye open across the summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Site in full sun with room to develop its full frame. Keep watered the first year and mulch to conserve moisture. Double white flowers with a red center make a showy late-summer display.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into sun or light shade.

directions: Give this variegated form full sun to light afternoon shade to keep leaf edges from scorching. Water evenly and mulch the roots. Creamy-margined foliage sets off lavender-blue flowers with a red eye through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a bright spot.

directions: Grow in full sun to part shade for the best cream-and-green leaf variegation. Water regularly and avoid drought stress that dulls the foliage. Semi-double purple buds that often stay partly closed accent the striking leaves.

common_name: Boule de Feu Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun in average well-drained soil and water to establish. Prune lightly in late winter to keep an even shape under heavy bloom. Full double violet-rose flowers cover the plant in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: Site in full sun with well-drained soil and water through the first summer. Cut back in late winter to keep the tall stems productive. Large violet-pink flowers with a deep red eye bloom heavily across summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant in full sun and give it space to reach a large mature size. Water evenly and mulch to keep the root zone moist. Big clear pink flowers with a red center bloom generously from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Grow in full sun in ordinary soil that drains well and water to establish. A late-winter prune improves flower size on new growth. Semi-double pink-red flowers appear in a long summer show.

planting.note: Plant in spring; ideal for narrow spaces.

directions: Use this narrow columnar form in tight spaces or as a flowering screen in full sun. Water to establish and prune only to tidy, since the upright habit needs little shaping. Purple-pink flowers with a red eye bloom along the whole slim column all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun so the cream-edged leaves color well and set off the flowers. Water to establish and mulch the base. Soft double pink flowers rise above blue-green foliage variegated with white, blooming through summer without setting seed.

common_name: Sugar Tip Gold Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: Grow in full sun to bring out the bright golden new foliage. Water evenly and avoid drought that stresses the colorful leaves. Double soft-pink flowers stand out against the yellow-gold leaves from midsummer on.

planting.note: Plant in spring; suited to small gardens and containers.

directions: Use this compact selection in small beds, low hedges, or large pots in full sun. Water regularly and mulch to keep the shallow roots moist. Small white flowers with a deep red eye stay open longer than usual and bloom all summer.

common_name: Lil' Kim Red Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Site this dwarf in full sun in a bed or container with good drainage. Keep evenly watered and mulch through the first season. Petite red-pink flowers with a darker eye open along the compact branches all summer.

common_name: Lil' Kim Violet Rose of Sharon

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny spot.

directions: Grow in full sun in well-drained soil and water to establish the small root system. Mulch to conserve moisture and prune only lightly. Small violet flowers with a red center bloom over a long summer season on a tidy plant.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this upright, narrow selection in full sun for screening or accent. Water to establish and mulch the root zone. Deep red-pink flowers with a dark eye bloom densely along the vertical branches through summer.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into full sun.

directions: Grow in full sun in average well-drained soil and water to establish. Prune lightly in late winter to keep the double flowers well displayed. Fully double raspberry-pink flowers bloom over a long stretch of summer.

common_name: Aaron's Beard (Rose of Sharon St. John's Wort)

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost as a spreading groundcover for banks and dry shade.

directions: Plant rooted divisions or cuttings at the depth they grew, spacing them close so the stoloniferous stems knit into a mat. Shear the whole planting to a few inches every couple of years in early spring to renew tired growth. Large lemon-yellow flowers with prominent stamen bosses open across summer.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or early autumn in well-drained soil.

directions: Dig a hole as deep as the rootball and twice as wide, then firm the plant in and water well its first season. Prune lightly in spring to shape and to encourage fresh flowering wood. Expect a long summer show of large golden saucer-shaped blooms up to two inches across.

planting.note: Compact rounded selection; plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Plant this neat, mounded selection level with the surrounding soil in a sunny, well-drained spot. It needs little pruning beyond removing any winter-killed tips in spring. Two-inch golden flowers with showy stamens cover the blue-green mound through summer.

planting.note: Cold-hardy North American native; plant in spring.

directions: Set this hardy native into average to moist but well-drained soil and give it room to form a tidy dome. Prune in early spring since flowers form on new growth. Clusters of yellow flowers with bushy stamens bloom in midsummer, followed by persistent seed capsules.

planting.note: Fine-textured upright selection; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this narrow-leaved selection in full sun for the heaviest flowering and best form. A hard prune in early spring keeps it dense and floriferous. It carries an unusually generous scattering of starry yellow flowers over fine green foliage all summer.

planting.note: Compact, cold-hardy selection; plant in spring.

directions: Give this dense, rounded selection a sunny site with sharp drainage. Renew it by pruning in early spring before growth resumes. Yellow midsummer flowers give way to reddish-brown capsules that hold on into winter for added interest.

planting.note: Woodland-edge shrub grown for its berries; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade where it tolerates dry spells once rooted. Cut back in early spring to keep it compact. Small yellow flowers are followed by berry-like capsules that ripen from red to glossy black and are prized in cut arrangements.

planting.note: Purple-flushed foliage form; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Grow in fertile, moisture-retentive soil where morning sun deepens the purple leaf tint. Prune hard in spring to encourage a flush of richly colored young foliage. Yellow flowers contrast with the plum-tinted leaves and mature into dark ornamental berries.

planting.note: Grown for its salmon-red berries; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in reasonably fertile, well-drained soil in sun or part shade and mulch to keep roots cool. Cut back in early spring to promote vigorous flowering stems. Yellow summer flowers give way to abundant coral to salmon-red berry capsules cherished by florists.

planting.note: Cut-flower berry selection; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Set into well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade for the heaviest berry set. Prune in early spring and remove any winter-damaged tips. Yellow flowers ripen into showy deep-red berry capsules that are widely used in the florist trade.

planting.note: Berry-producing florist selection; plant in spring or fall.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained ground and water through dry spells while it establishes. An early-spring prune keeps growth fresh and productive. Golden flowers are followed by large glossy berry capsules that color richly for cutting.

planting.note: Low, arching hybrid; plant after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant this low, arching hybrid in well-drained soil where its reddish stems can spill gracefully. In colder zones it may die back in winter but regrows readily from the base after a spring cutback. Large yellow flowers with reddish-tinged stamens bloom from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Variegated tender selection; plant after the last frost.

directions: Grow this variegated form in part shade to protect the pink-and-cream leaf margins from scorch. Provide shelter and a winter mulch since it is less hardy than the plain species. Yellow flowers appear against the tricolor foliage from summer onward.

planting.note: Semi-evergreen shrub; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this semi-evergreen shrub in well-drained soil in sun or light shade with some wind shelter in colder gardens. Prune in spring to keep it shapely and to spur new flowering wood. Cup-shaped golden flowers with a central tuft of stamens open through summer and early fall.

planting.note: Rounded arching shrub with red capsules; plant in spring.

directions: Give this dependable shrub room to arch and mound in a sunny, well-drained bed. A light spring trim maintains the rounded outline. Large bright yellow flowers with long spidery stamens carry on for months, followed by bronze-red seed capsules.

planting.note: Tender-ish evergreen shrub for mild gardens; plant after frost.

directions: Plant this semi-evergreen species in well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered position. Trim after flowering to keep it neat. Its golden flowers are notable for the very long, exserted stamens that give a delicate, whiskered look through summer.

planting.note: Compact rock-garden subshrub; plant in spring.

directions: Tuck this dwarf subshrub into a sunny rock garden or wall crevice with very sharp drainage. It resents wet feet, so add grit to heavy soils. Comparatively large golden flowers crown the low gray-green mound in summer.

planting.note: Upright native for damp ground; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this tall, upright native in average to moist soil where it tolerates occasional flooding. Thin older stems in early spring to keep growth open. Dense clusters of small yellow flowers smother the fine-textured shrub in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Hardy semi-evergreen shrub with autumn color; plant in spring.

directions: Grow this reliable semi-evergreen in well-drained soil in sun or part shade. A spring prune keeps it bushy and encourages new bloom wood. Golden bowl-shaped flowers appear through summer, and the foliage often flushes red in autumn.

planting.note: Large-flowered but tender hybrid; plant after frost in a sheltered spot.

directions: Give this tall, tender hybrid a warm, sheltered wall and rich, well-drained soil. Protect the base with mulch in cold winters as it may be cut back by frost. It bears some of the largest golden flowers of any St. John's wort in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Arching semi-evergreen shrub; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this arching semi-evergreen in well-drained soil in full sun for the best flowering. Prune in early spring to renew the graceful, spreading stems. Star-shaped golden flowers are carried along the branches through the summer months.

planting.note: Chinese shrub with red-tinted buds; plant in spring.

directions: Site this Chinese species in well-drained soil in a sunny, open position. A light spring prune maintains a tidy, bushy habit. Red-flushed buds open to golden star-shaped flowers over reddish young stems in summer.

planting.note: Southeastern US native for wet sites; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this southeastern native along pond edges or in consistently moist soil where many shrubs struggle. It also adapts to average garden beds with regular water. Fine willowy foliage sets off small yellow flowers borne along the stems in summer.

planting.note: Low, softly hairy rock-garden subshrub; plant in spring.

directions: Plant this low, gray-green subshrub in gritty, free-draining soil in a rock garden or raised bed. Cut back lightly after bloom to keep the mound compact. A profusion of bright golden flowers nearly hides the soft foliage in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Vigorous semi-evergreen Chinese species; plant in spring.

directions: Grow this vigorous semi-evergreen in fertile, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Prune in early spring to control size and refresh the flowering wood. Large deep-yellow bowl-shaped flowers open over arching branches through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for maximum flowering.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and water the first year to root in. An upright, robust shrub smothered in bright yellow flowers all summer. Thin out older wood in early spring to maintain vigor and shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give some afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Plant in cool, well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. Low and spreading, it carries orange-red flowers that hold color best out of blazing afternoon sun. Shear lightly in spring; blooms run through summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and water to establish. This selection carries oversized bright yellow flowers on a rounded, tidy shrub. A light spring trim maintains the shape; bloom continues from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring; cooler weather keeps the pink strong.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water evenly the first year. Large pink flowers with a paler center hold color best where nights stay cool. Shear lightly in early spring to keep the mound dense and flowering all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; orange color is richest in cool conditions.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and keep moisture steady through establishment. Warm orange flowers glow best in cool weather and light afternoon shade. Trim lightly each spring; flowering carries from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full to part sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water the first season. Flowers open orange-red and soften to yellow tones, giving a warm two-tone effect on a compact plant. A light spring shearing keeps it neat; blooms run through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; cooler weather keeps the pink vivid.

directions: Plant in free-draining soil and water evenly until established. Deep pink flowers cover a low, compact mound and hold best in cool weather. Trim lightly in early spring to keep the plant tidy and flowering into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; more upright than most.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water to establish. This tall, upright form has striking silvery-gray foliage and pale creamy-yellow flowers. Thin out old stems in early spring to keep it vigorous; blooms open through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; an early and long bloomer.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water through the first summer. Bright yellow flowers open early and keep coming into fall on a rounded shrub. Shear lightly in early spring to maintain a full, even shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a low, spreading groundcover form.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and water to establish. This low, mat-forming selection bears sulphur-yellow flowers over blue-green leaves and works well at the front of a border. A light spring trim keeps it dense; bloom lasts all summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; cooler weather keeps the pink from bleaching.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and keep evenly moist. Pale pink flowers with a darker center hold color best in cool weather and light shade, fading toward white in heat. Shear lightly in spring for a compact, free-flowering mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring; color richest in cool weather and light shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and provide steady moisture. Bright orange flowers with a pale center hold their color best out of intense afternoon sun. Trim lightly in early spring; blooms carry from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water to establish. Soft pale-yellow flowers glow against gray-green foliage on a rounded, bushy shrub. Shear lightly each spring; the long bloom runs from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a low, silvery groundcover form.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water to establish. This low, mounding variety has silvery-gray, hairy leaves and white flowers, making a fine dwarf groundcover. Shear lightly in spring; expect white bloom through the summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun.

directions: Set in well-drained soil and water through the first summer. An upright, vigorous shrub bearing clear yellow flowers over fresh green foliage. Thin out older wood in early spring; flowering continues all season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; naturally rounded, ball-shaped habit.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil and water to establish. This selection forms a naturally neat, rounded shrub covered in golden-yellow flowers. Little pruning is needed beyond a light spring tidy; bloom runs summer to fall.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or take hardwood cuttings in late winter.

directions: Plant in consistently moist soil at the same depth the shrub grew in the nursery and water well the first season. Cut a third of the oldest stems to the ground each late winter to keep the newest wood, which shows the brightest red bark. Flat clusters of small creamy-white flowers open in late spring, followed by white berries.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring where the winter stems will catch low sun.

directions: Site in moist ground and keep it damp through the first summer while roots establish. Remove the oldest quarter of the canes at ground level each spring because young wood shows the strongest chartreuse-yellow winter color. White spring flower clusters give way to pale berries that birds strip quickly.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in moist soil for the best cherry-red winter stems.

directions: Dig a hole twice the root width in moisture-retentive soil and firm the plant in gently. Coppice the oldest stems hard in late winter to force the bright cherry-red new growth this selection is grown for. Small white flower clusters appear in late spring above the green foliage.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring; tolerates wet sites well.

directions: Set into moist to wet soil and mulch to hold moisture during establishment. Renewal-prune the oldest third of the stems each spring to maintain the deep red winter bark. Expect flat white flower heads in late spring and burgundy fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; this is a compact, dense form good for smaller beds.

directions: Plant in moist soil and water through the first growing season. Its naturally compact habit needs less pruning, but thinning old canes in late winter keeps the bark vivid red. White flower clusters open in late spring on this rounded, twiggy shrub.

planting.note: Plant in spring as a low groundcover or front-of-border filler.

directions: Space plants closely in moist soil to knit into a low mat. Shear lightly in early spring to tidy the fine twigs, which show red-and-green winter tips. Flowering is sparse on this dwarf, so it is grown chiefly for its compact form and winter stem color.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this dwarf red-twig suits smaller gardens and containers.

directions: Plant in moist soil and keep evenly watered while establishing. Its compact habit needs only light thinning of old stems in late winter to keep the red bark bright. Small white spring flowers are followed by good burgundy fall color on this half-size selection.

planting.note: Plant in spring where afternoon shade will protect the variegated leaves.

directions: Give this variegated yellow-twig moist soil and some midday shade to keep the white leaf margins from scorching. Thin the oldest canes each late winter to promote the bright golden-yellow new bark. Cream-white flower clusters open in late spring against the green-and-white foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground with some shade to prevent leaf burn.

directions: Set in moist soil and shelter from harsh afternoon sun so the gold leaf edges stay crisp. Coppice a portion of the old stems in late winter for the reddest winter bark. Flat white flower heads appear in late spring above the yellow-margined leaves.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring in most soils, including damp ground.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in moist, well-drained soil and water through the first season. Cut the oldest quarter to third of stems to the base each spring to keep bright red winter bark. Small yellowish-white flower clusters open in late spring, followed by pale blue-white berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring where light shade will keep the white-edged leaves fresh.

directions: Grow in moist soil with protection from hot afternoon sun to avoid scorching the cream leaf margins. Renewal-prune old stems in late winter to keep the red winter bark and encourage lush variegated growth. Small white spring flowers stand out against the gray-green and white foliage.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for the most vivid coral-red winter stems.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil and water while establishing. Hard-prune the oldest stems each late winter because the brightest coral-red bark forms on one-year-old wood. White flower clusters open in late spring and are followed by whitish berries and red-purple fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this compact variegated form fits smaller beds.

directions: Plant in moist soil with a little afternoon shade to protect the white-margined leaves. Thin old stems in late winter to keep the red winter bark and dense habit. Small white flower clusters open in late spring on this more compact selection.

planting.note: Plant in spring where light shade keeps the gold-and-pink leaves from fading.

directions: Set in moist, fertile soil with dappled afternoon shade to preserve the yellow and pink leaf variegation. Prune old canes in late winter to hold the red winter bark and fresh foliage. Creamy-white flower clusters appear in late spring on the arching stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring with light shade to keep the golden leaves from scorching.

directions: Grow in moist soil and give afternoon shade so the soft yellow foliage does not burn. Cut back old stems in late winter for red winter bark and the brightest new leaf color. Small white flower clusters open in late spring above the golden leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring; grown for unusual dark purple-black winter stems.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil and water while young. Thin the oldest stems each late winter to encourage the deep blackish-purple new bark this cultivar is prized for. White flower clusters open in late spring, and the foliage turns purple-red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where light shade protects the gold-edged foliage.

directions: Plant in moist soil and shelter from harsh sun to keep the bright golden leaf margins clean. Prune out old stems in late winter for strong red winter bark and vigorous variegated shoots. Creamy-white flowers open in late spring above the yellow-edged leaves.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring; tolerant of a wide range of soils.

directions: Plant at nursery depth in most soils and water while establishing. Coppice the oldest stems in late winter to renew the dark blood-red winter bark. Flat clusters of dull-white flowers open in late spring and give way to black berries relished by birds.

planting.note: Plant in spring in an open, sunny spot to show the fiery winter stems.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil and site where winter light can backlight the stems. Cut roughly a third of the oldest canes to the base each spring to keep the yellow, orange, and red winter color intense. Small white flower clusters appear in late spring, and leaves turn yellow in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the brightest multi-colored winter bark.

directions: Set in moist, well-drained soil and grow in full sun for the strongest stem color. Remove the oldest stems in early spring to encourage fresh shoots that glow orange-yellow to red in winter. White spring flower clusters are followed by dark berries.

planting.note: Plant in spring as an unusual upright, compact accent shrub.

directions: Plant in moist soil in a spot where its stiff, upright form stands out. The crowded, crinkled dark green leaves need little pruning beyond removing dead wood. This slow, columnar selection is grown mainly for its distinctive congested habit rather than showy bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet or streamside ground where it thrives.

directions: Plant in moist to wet soil, making it a strong choice for rain gardens and pond edges. Thin old stems in late winter to keep the reddish-purple winter bark bright. Flat clusters of yellowish-white flowers open in early summer, followed by porcelain-blue berries.

planting.note: Plant in early spring as a large shrub or small multi-stemmed tree.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in sun or light shade and water through the first seasons. Little pruning is needed beyond shaping and removing crossing branches after flowering. Small yellow flower clusters bloom on bare stems in very early spring, followed by edible tart red fruits in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; blooms even earlier than common cornelian cherry.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to light shade and keep watered while establishing. Prune lightly after bloom to shape the multi-stemmed frame and reveal the exfoliating bark. Clusters of yellow flowers open on bare branches in late winter, followed by glossy red fruits.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a yellow-stemmed form with better disease resistance.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil and water while establishing. Cut back the oldest stems in late winter to keep the bright yellow winter bark on young wood. Small white flower clusters open in late spring above green summer foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest orange winter stems.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile soil in full sun to bring out the orange-red winter color. Coppice the oldest stems in early spring so fresh, brightly colored shoots keep coming. Yellow-green spring foliage and small white flower clusters give way to fiery orange stems once the leaves drop.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a denser, more restrained red-twig for tighter spaces.

directions: Plant in moist soil and keep watered the first season. Its dense, compact habit needs only light thinning of old canes in late winter to hold the red winter bark. Small white flower clusters appear in late spring on this tidy selection.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring or in fall while the soil is still workable.

directions: Plant in well-drained, even lean soil at the same depth it grew in the pot, since rich or soggy ground weakens color and habit. Water through the first season, then let it fend for itself, and prune hard in late winter if you want a tidier shape. The airy pink-to-tan flower panicles open in early summer and give the plant its smoky haze.

planting.note: Plant dormant nursery stock in early spring on a well-drained site with room to grow.

directions: Give this native its space, planting on lean, rocky, or alkaline ground where drainage is sharp. It needs little supplemental water once rooted and resents heavy feeding. Sparse smoky plumes appear in early summer, but the real show is brilliant orange-to-red fall color that outdoes most of its relatives.

planting.note: Plant container or dormant stock in early spring or fall in full sun.

directions: Site this vigorous hybrid in full sun and lean, well-drained soil so its wine-red new leaves color strongly. Cut it back hard in late winter to keep it shrubby and to force larger, more richly hued foliage. Big soft-pink smoke plumes form in summer, followed by fiery scarlet autumn leaves.

planting.note: Set out in early spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant in full sun and unimproved, well-drained soil to bring out the deep blackish-purple leaf color, which fades toward green in shade. For the most intense foliage, coppice the plant to a low framework each late winter, accepting fewer flowers in trade. Left unpruned it carries smoky purple-pink plumes in summer and turns red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun for best leaf color.

directions: Choose a bright, well-drained spot so the dark maroon-purple foliage holds its color deep into the season. Keep feeding and watering modest, since lush growth muddies the leaf tone. It reddens vividly in autumn and, if left unpruned, floats hazy purplish plumes in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall with some afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Give this golden-leaved form a spot with morning sun but shelter from harsh afternoon rays, which can scorch the bright foliage. Even, moderate moisture keeps the chartreuse-to-gold leaves fresh through summer. Foliage shifts to orange, red, and amber in fall, and pinkish smoke plumes appear on unpruned plants.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in full sun to encourage heavy flowering.

directions: Plant in full sun on lean, well-drained soil to reward its reputation as an exceptionally free-flowering, even young, smokebush. Green summer leaves set off an unusually generous cloud of pinkish-green plumes that can appear on quite small plants. Minimal pruning keeps the bloom show going, and foliage colors up in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; noted for cold hardiness in northern gardens.

directions: Set this hardy purple-leaved selection in full sun and well-drained soil, where it stands up to cold better than many cultivars. Purplish-red new leaves mature toward green-purple, and modest feeding keeps color strong. Smoky purple-pink plumes arrive in summer, with red-orange fall color to follow.

planting.note: Plant in early spring or fall in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun on poor-to-average, well-drained ground to deepen the dark maroon-purple foliage of this old English selection. Hold back on rich feeding, which can wash out leaf color and encourage floppy growth. Purple-pink flower haze forms in summer and leaves flame red before dropping in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun; well suited to smaller gardens.

directions: This compact selection fits small spaces, so plant it in full sun and well-drained soil at its original depth. New growth emerges deep purple-black, and a hard late-winter cutback keeps it dense and intensifies leaf color. Green-tinged smoke plumes appear in summer, and foliage turns red-purple in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall with light afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Set this golden-leaved compact form where it gets bright light but escapes the hottest afternoon sun, which can scorch the foliage. New leaves flush orange before settling to gold, and even moisture keeps them clean. It shows warm orange-red fall color and stays neat with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the heaviest smoke display.

directions: Plant this green-leaved type in full sun and lean, well-drained soil for a dependable, heavy show of smoky plumes. Leave it largely unpruned so the abundant pinkish flower panicles can develop across the whole crown. Summer smoke gives way to yellow, orange, and red autumn foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun; naturally dense and rounded.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, where this selection forms a naturally compact, bushy mound needing little shaping. Green summer foliage carries a generous haze of beige-pink smoke plumes. Leaves warm to orange and red in fall, closing out the season on a bright note.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to maximize the flower display.

directions: Site in full sun and average, well-drained soil, and go easy on pruning so it can flower heavily. Green leaves set off large, soft pink-flushed smoke plumes that mature to a champagne tone. Fall brings a mix of yellow and red foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun with room to reach small-tree size.

directions: Give this vigorous hybrid full sun and well-drained soil, allowing space since it can grow tree-like if left unpruned. Green summer leaves carry big pink smoke plumes across a broad crown. It earns its name with brilliant orange-red fall color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the deepest foliage color.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil to darken the near-black purple foliage of this selection. Modest feeding and watering keep the color dense and the habit compact. Smoky pink-purple plumes rise in summer, and leaves turn fiery in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun; ideal for small beds and containers.

directions: This true dwarf suits small gardens and large pots, so plant it in full sun and sharp-draining soil. Deep purple leaves stay richly colored on a naturally tight, low mound needing little pruning. Small smoke plumes may appear in summer, and foliage reddens in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the fullest smoke display.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, and leave it largely unpruned so it can bloom to its potential. This green-leaved form is prized for an especially dense, long-lasting cloud of pinkish smoke plumes. Foliage takes on yellow-to-red tones in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun to keep the purple leaf tone.

directions: Plant this old purple-leaved form in full sun and lean, well-drained soil so the reddish-purple foliage holds well before greening in summer. Avoid heavy feeding, which dilutes the color. Purple-pink smoke plumes form in summer and foliage flares red in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in full sun for the pink-tinged smoke effect.

directions: This purple-plumed botanical variety wants full sun and well-drained, unimproved soil to perform its best. Its green leaves are secondary to the rosy-purple flower panicles that give an especially smoky summer effect. Prune lightly if at all, and enjoy warm fall foliage color.

planting.note: Plant dormant or container stock in early spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Plant this less-common Asian species in full sun and well-drained soil, giving it space to reach a large shrub or small tree. It is drought tolerant once established and tolerates poor ground. Airy summer plumes are followed by good fall color on its blue-green leaves.

planting.note: Set out container or bare-root plants in spring once the ground has thawed.

directions: Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball and set the plant at the depth it grew in the pot. Water regularly the first season, then let it fend for itself as it tolerates drought well. Small yellow flowers hang beneath the branches in mid-spring, followed by red berries that persist into winter. Prune after flowering to shape the dense, thorny form. Note that this species is invasive in parts of the eastern United States and banned in several states.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown shrubs in spring in a spot with strong sun for best color.

directions: Plant this compact mound in full sun so the deep crimson-purple foliage keeps its rich tone. Keep the base clear of mulch piled against the stems and water through the first summer. Tiny yellow blooms appear in spring but the plant is grown mainly for its color. A light shearing after bloom tidies the tight rounded shape.

planting.note: Set out in spring where afternoon sun will bring out the mottled new growth.

directions: Give this shrub full sun so the new leaves emerge rose-pink flecked with cream against the purple older foliage. Plant at the same depth as the nursery pot and water until established. Yellow spring flowers give way to red autumn berries. Prune lightly after flowering to encourage the colorful fresh growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring with a little shade from harsh afternoon sun to avoid leaf scorch.

directions: Site this yellow-leaved form where it gets bright light but is shielded from scorching midday sun. Set it at the depth it grew in the container and water regularly the first year. The chartreuse-to-gold foliage is the main show, with small yellow flowers in spring. Trim after bloom to keep the mound compact.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest purple leaf color.

directions: Set this very compact selection in full sun to keep the deep royal-purple foliage saturated. Plant level with the surrounding soil and water through the first season. It rarely needs pruning thanks to its naturally tight, dense habit. Small yellow flowers appear in spring beneath the branches.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to fuel the bright coral-orange new growth.

directions: Use this narrow, upright form as a vertical accent or a tight low hedge. Plant at the nursery depth and water until roots take hold. New leaves emerge coral-orange, mature to burgundy, and turn ruby-red in fall. Little pruning is needed to hold its upright column.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to keep the leaf margins brightly edged.

directions: Set this tidy mound in full sun so the red-orange leaves keep their thin gold margin. Plant level with the soil surface and water regularly the first summer. The dense, rounded shape needs almost no pruning. Foliage deepens to a fiery red in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to develop the brightest gold foliage.

directions: Plant this narrow gold-leaved column where full sun brings out the yellow color without heavy scorch. Set it at container depth and water the first season. New growth carries orange-red tips over the gold leaves, and fall brings ruby tones. Its upright form makes a striking vertical accent with minimal pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed for the fullest gold color.

directions: This tiny golden mound suits the front of a border or a low edge. Plant at soil level and keep watered until established. The small leaves emerge orange-tinged and settle to bright gold through summer. Its compact habit means it seldom needs shaping.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to hold the upright form and dark color.

directions: Use this strongly upright purple form as an exclamation point in a border. Set it at nursery depth and water until roots establish. The deep wine-purple leaves hold color all season and redden in autumn. Prune only to remove stray shoots that break the narrow column.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest reddish-purple foliage.

directions: Plant this classic purple-leaved barberry in full sun where its color develops best. Set it at the depth it grew in the pot and water through the first year. Yellow spring flowers hang below the arching branches, followed by red fall berries. Shear after flowering to maintain a rounded hedge or specimen.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this species is one of the most cold-hardy barberries.

directions: Set this hardy species in full sun to light shade and water while it establishes. Fragrant yellow flowers cluster along the branches in spring, followed by hanging red berries and brilliant red fall foliage. Give it room since it can sucker into a thicket. Prune out old canes in late winter to keep it open.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot to protect evergreen leaves from winter wind.

directions: Plant this evergreen species where it gets sun to part shade and shelter from drying winter wind. Set it at nursery depth and water regularly until established. Fragrant yellow flowers open in spring, followed by blue-black berries, and the spiny leaves make a formidable barrier hedge. Prune after flowering to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring; this hybrid tolerates heat, drought, and cold well.

directions: This tough hybrid makes an excellent dense barrier hedge in sun or light shade. Plant at container depth and water the first season, after which it handles heat and drought. Leaves stay green or semi-evergreen and turn reddish in fall. Yellow spring flowers are followed by dark red berries; shear to shape as needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a spot sheltered from harsh winter wind.

directions: Plant this compact evergreen with glossy dark leaves in sun to part shade. Set it at the nursery depth and water until established. Golden-yellow flowers appear in spring, and the undersides of the leaves show a striking silvery-white. Its neat mounded shape rarely needs more than light tidying.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered site for this low evergreen mound.

directions: Use this dense low evergreen as groundcover or a front-of-border mound in sun or part shade. Plant at container depth and water while it settles in. The small glossy leaves are white-frosted beneath, and bright yellow flowers dot the plant in spring. It spreads slowly into a broad, weed-smothering carpet.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade for this compact green form.

directions: This dwarf green-leaved barberry forms a tight, rounded cushion good for low hedging. Plant at soil level and water through the first summer. The bright green leaves turn orange-red in fall, and small yellow flowers appear in spring. Its dense habit takes shearing well.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to hold the deep red foliage.

directions: Site this compact red-leaved form in full sun for the richest color. Plant at the depth it grew in the pot and water the first season. The reddish-purple leaves deepen through summer and glow scarlet in fall. It keeps a neat rounded shape with only occasional trimming.

planting.note: Plant in spring where bright light brings out the gold but midday scorch is eased.

directions: This low gold-leaved mound resists sun scorch better than many yellow forms. Plant at soil level and water until established. The bright golden foliage holds through summer and needs no regular pruning. Its spreading, compact habit suits edges and mass plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the two-tone orange and chartreuse leaves.

directions: Plant this colorful form in full sun so new coral-orange leaves stand out against the chartreuse older growth. Set it at nursery depth and water the first year. It resists scorch well for a bright-leaved barberry. A light shearing keeps the rounded shape tidy.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the deepest burgundy foliage.

directions: This small burgundy-leaved barberry fits tight spaces and low edging. Plant it level with the soil and water through the first summer. The rich red-purple foliage holds all season with little care. Its dense dwarf habit means pruning is rarely needed.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered spot; hardy only in milder zones.

directions: Plant this handsome evergreen where it gets sun to part shade and shelter from cold wind. Set it at nursery depth and water while it establishes. Masses of deep orange-yellow flowers cover the plant in spring, followed by blue-purple berries. Prune lightly after flowering to keep it shapely.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade with room for the arching branches.

directions: Give this large arching evergreen plenty of room for its cascading branches. Plant at container depth and water until established. Golden-yellow flowers wreath the stems in spring, making a spectacular fountain of color. Prune after flowering to control size and renew growth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the best fruit display.

directions: Plant this mounding species in full sun where its autumn berries will shine. Set it at nursery depth and water while it establishes. Yellow summer flowers give way to abundant coral-pink to salmon berries that persist into winter. The small leaves color richly in fall; prune lightly to shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to bring out the red leaves with gold margins.

directions: This compact form shows red leaves edged in bright gold, most vivid in full sun. Plant it at soil level and water through the first season. The low, dense mound stays tidy without much pruning. Foliage intensifies to fiery tones in autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; this hybrid is very cold-hardy.

directions: This tough hybrid barberry makes a rounded green hedge with strong fall color. Plant at container depth and water the first year. Fragrant yellow flowers in spring are followed by red berries and orange-to-red autumn foliage. It shears well for hedging.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for bold golden-yellow foliage.

directions: Plant this upright gold-leaved form in full sun to keep the yellow color bright. Set it at nursery depth and water through the first summer. New growth carries orange tints over the golden leaves. Prune after bloom to maintain the rounded, slightly upright shape.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to develop the chartreuse and pink new growth.

directions: Site this bright form in full sun where new leaves emerge lime-green flushed with pink and cream. Plant at container depth and water while it settles. The color softens to chartreuse through summer and reddens in fall. Its compact rounded habit needs little pruning.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the strongest pink mottling.

directions: Plant this vigorous form in full sun so leaves show their marbling of pink, white, and grey over red-purple. Set it at nursery depth and water the first year. The mottled color is most vivid on new growth. Prune after flowering to encourage fresh, colorful shoots.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to hold the narrow form and red color.

directions: This narrow upright form works as a vertical accent or a tight screen. Plant at nursery depth and water until roots take hold. The purple-red leaves deepen to scarlet in autumn. Little pruning is needed to keep the slender column.
